lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms principally providing outsourced computer systems planning, architecture, configuration, network design, systems integration, implementation, installation, user training, and related support to external customers through recurring engagements or repeat project work.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal IT units, pure hardware or packaged-software resellers, software publishers, firms principally classified as custom programmers or facilities managers, temporary staffing businesses, shells, one-off practices without repeat outsourced service, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers are businesses, nonprofits, and government agencies buying expertise to assess needs and integrate hardware, software, networks, security, and data. Revenue commonly combines time-and-materials professional services, fixed-fee projects, milestone or unit pricing, hardware and software pass-through, and smaller support or maintenance streams; repeat work follows platform upgrades, integrations, migrations, security changes, and continuing customer roadmaps.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations and wages for four-digit NAICS 5415 rather than six-digit NAICS 541512, and Anthropic measures selected interactions with one AI vendor rather than wage-weighted task exposure. The bridge uses the BLS occupation mix and published occupation wages to emphasize costly computer, management, and business work, then maps documented duties to current AI-suitable work such as code generation, testing, documentation, research, support triage, data transformation, and project administration. Inclusion of custom programming in NAICS 5415 likely overstates coding exposure for systems design, while omission of firm-specific administrative workflows may understate exposure.
a.rationale: Computer and mathematical occupations account for 50.57 percent of employment in the broader industry, including software developers at 21.62 percent, computer support at 7.51 percent, and systems analysts at 4.62 percent; management and business occupations add substantial high-wage labor (S3). BLS duties show automatable artifacts in requirements analysis, diagrams, configuration, code, tests, defect reports, documentation, research, and user materials, alongside harder customer consultation, architecture, security judgment, coordination, and deployment accountability (S4, S5). Anthropic found coding use highly concentrated and classified 79 percent of sampled coding-agent interactions as automation, but its population is self-selected (S7). The range excludes work already automated and preserves a large human share for customer context, architecture tradeoffs, stakeholder alignment, physical installation, and production responsibility.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study across independent systems integrators should map current hours by occupation and task, distinguish already automated work, and test representative requirements, configuration, migration, coding, testing, documentation, support, and project-management tasks with production AI tools.
caveats: The available occupation profile is for NAICS 5415 and includes custom programming, facilities management, and other computer services.
Employment shares are not wage shares; published occupation wages only approximate the weighting required by this primitive.
Observed AI conversation patterns show use, not the share of all paid work that is technically substitutable.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input is six-digit and rated medium evidence, but its wage and receipts vintages differ and its harmonization factor adds measurement error.

rho.bridge: The Census adoption study covers U.S. firms across sectors, Anthropic covers users of its own coding products, and METR studies experienced open-source developers rather than lower-middle-market systems-design firms. Current use, interaction-level automation, and developer speed are translated into the share of exposed labor that can be embedded in reliable client delivery within five years. The technically skilled workforce and digital work product imply faster implementation than the all-firm Census average, while vendor selection, security, legacy-system context, quality assurance, and client approval pull the estimate below coding-agent automation rates. Vendor usage data may overstate adoption among ordinary firms; broad Census data may understate this technology-native industry.
rho.rationale: Census found AI use in 18 percent of firms and 32 percent on an employment-weighted basis, but 66 percent of users reported augmentation only and employment decreases were rare, showing that adoption does not equal labor realization (S6). Anthropic coding-agent interactions demonstrate substantial task delegation but much lower enterprise representation than startup representation (S7). METR's early study found a developer slowdown, while its later data suggested improvement but was too selection-biased for a reliable effect estimate (S8). Systems-design firms possess the technical talent to integrate tools quickly, yet client confidentiality, legacy dependencies, testing, cybersecurity, and accountable release processes prevent full implementation.
rho.change_evidence: A matched sample of systems integrators should report deployed tools, seat utilization, task-level hours before and after adoption, rework and defect escape rates, client restrictions, review intensity, training cost, and realized hiring avoidance across completed customer engagements.
caveats: No source measures five-year implementation of exposed labor specifically for NAICS 541512.
Product telemetry is selected toward willing users and does not establish net productivity or headcount effects.
Firm-level AI adoption can remain confined to marketing or internal research rather than billable delivery.
Implementation will vary with customer regulation, data sensitivity, platform maturity, codebase quality, and contract terms.

e.rationale: The six-digit definition is inherently an external expertise and integration service, and Census distinguishes it from custom programming, facilities management, software publishing, and pure resale (S1, S2). The survey form identifies business and government customer classes and service outputs spanning packaged-software integration, databases, networks, security, system design, development, and integration (S2). Most scaled independent firms should therefore satisfy the outsourced-service lens, with exclusions for hardware-led resellers, captive support units, staffing-heavy firms, nonrepeat project shops, and businesses whose economics belong in adjacent codes.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit firm roster enriched with independent ownership, service-line revenue, repeat-customer revenue, hardware and software pass-through, staffing exposure, customer concentration, and normalized EBITDA would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: NAICS classification does not reveal repeat revenue, owner dependence, or whether a firm's delivery organization is transferable.
The Census survey lists products associated with the broader industry form and does not publish the six-digit revenue mix on that page.
The frozen LMM firm count is margin-bridged from size classes rather than directly observed, so the eligible-firm count inherits margin, firm-size, and classification error.

s5.bridge: Census owner-age evidence covers U.S. employer-business owners broadly, while PwC and LSEG deal evidence covers global IT services transactions and is tilted toward much larger deals. Older ownership is treated as succession pressure, and adjacent IT-services deal activity as evidence that control transfers can clear. Both can overstate qualifying LMM transfers because retirement may lead to closure or internal succession and large firms are more marketable; the broad deal data can understate unannounced small private transactions.
s5.rationale: Census reports that more than half of responding owners of employer businesses were at least age 55, establishing broad succession pressure but not sale intent (S11). PwC reports that IT services increased its share of technology deal volume to 18 percent in 2024 and expected continued sector activity, demonstrating buyer interest in the adjacent transaction market (S12). The base case allows for active consolidation and a large potential firm universe but applies a material haircut for internal transfers, closures, owner-dependent delivery, weak documentation, customer concentration, and firms that never enter a sale process.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year database of U.S. systems-design control transactions, failed sale processes, closures, internal successions, and recapitalizations linked to eligible-firm counts, EBITDA, owner age, customer concentration, and recurring revenue would materially change this probability.
caveats: Broad owner age does not measure intent, timing, transferable value, or a qualifying control transfer.
The M&A evidence is global, spans IT services beyond NAICS 541512, and emphasizes large transactions.
Many small technology-services acquisitions are private and unannounced, while headline activity lacks an eligible-firm denominator.

q.bridge: Perficient is a large public U.S. IT consultancy rather than a lower-middle-market NAICS 541512 firm, but its contract disclosures directly illustrate adjacent professional-services billing. Its 2023 time-and-materials revenue and fixed-fee revenue are used to anchor a mixed billing model, then lower retention is assigned to hourly work and higher initial retention to fixed-fee work before allowing for renewals and competition. The proxy may understate retention if smaller integrators use more fixed-scope or value pricing and hardware margins, or overstate it if they function mainly as hourly subcontractors with concentrated customers.
q.rationale: Perficient disclosed that most projects were time-and-materials and reported 668.7 million dollars of that revenue against 896.7 million dollars of professional-services fees in 2023; fixed-fee and fixed-fee-percent-complete work made up most of the remainder (S13). Hourly contracts pass faster completion to the client through fewer billable hours unless the provider redeploys capacity or expands scope. Fixed-fee projects can retain near-term savings, but rebids, rate negotiation, vendor comparisons, offshore competition, and customers' own AI capabilities share the benefit over time. The base case therefore retains well under half of implemented gross benefit.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract sample should measure revenue and gross profit by time-and-materials, fixed-fee, milestone, unit, managed-service, and resale terms, then compare realized hours, scope expansion, utilization, renewal pricing, win rates, and customer concessions after automation.
caveats: A single large consultancy may not represent LMM systems integrators or their service mix.
Revenue-recognition categories do not measure customer sharing or the discounted share of savings ultimately retained.
Hardware and software resale margins, vendor rebates, and managed support can change retention but are not isolated here.
This primitive addresses price and benefit sharing only; demand growth and self-service are handled separately.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output and employment for four-digit NAICS 5415, not constant-quality demand for the current six-digit NAICS 541512 service basket. Applying the published 3.2 percent annual real-output growth rate for five years yields approximately 1.17, while the range allows systems design to lag or exceed its broader industry. Broader NAICS coverage can overstate growth if custom programming and other computer services grow faster, while rapid integration demand from AI, cloud, and cybersecurity can make the four-digit average conservative for systems design.
d5.rationale: BLS projects NAICS 5415 output to rise at a 3.2 percent compound annual rate in chained dollars and places the industry among the fastest-growing output categories (S10). It separately projects 15.8 percent employment growth for computer systems design and related services and attributes demand to IT infrastructure, custom solutions, cybersecurity, cloud, and AI implementation (S9). Systems analysts are also expected to be hired as organizations expand IT, and BLS notes that small firms may contract with outside providers rather than hire directly (S4). These signals support strong real demand growth despite technology cycles and periodic project deferrals.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted receipts and project-volume data for NAICS 541512, split by integration, advisory, implementation, security, network, and resale work, together with backlog, bookings, customer IT budgets, and outsourcing rates, would replace the four-digit proxy.
caveats: The BLS projections are four-digit and cannot isolate computer systems design from adjacent computer services.
Real industry output is not a direct constant-quality quantity measure and may reflect mix shifts toward higher-value services.
Demand is cyclical with enterprise technology budgets and can be delayed by financing conditions, platform transitions, or customer uncertainty.

o.rationale: Customer-specific systems still require an accountable party to elicit requirements, choose architecture, configure and connect platforms, migrate data, validate security and performance, manage cutover, train users, and resolve failures. Census defines the industry around integrated systems and notes that providers often install systems and train or support users, while BLS describes systems analysts as configuring, overseeing installation, testing, and translating between managers and technical teams (S1, S4). AI, low-code tools, packaged connectors, vendor professional services, and in-house technical teams can eliminate simpler engagements, but they are less likely to remove accountability for complex legacy, regulated, or mission-critical integrations.
o.change_evidence: Interviews with CIOs, IT directors, procurement leaders, platform vendors, and systems integrators should quantify which current deliverables will be self-served, purchased as software, moved to hyperscalers, retained in-house, or still require an independent accountable integrator at year five.
caveats: No source directly forecasts the operator-required share for NAICS 541512.
Operator need varies sharply between standardized cloud deployments and complex legacy, security-sensitive, or regulated integrations.
Some demand may shift to software vendors, hyperscalers, offshore providers, or customer employees rather than remain with lens firms.

narrative.executive_view: Computer systems design combines a very labor-intensive cost base with digital, AI-compatible production work and strong projected demand for cloud, cybersecurity, AI, and systems integration. The opportunity is not simple replacement of integrators: it is an AI-enabled delivery platform that compresses coding, testing, documentation, configuration, support, and project overhead while preserving customer-specific architecture and accountable implementation. The main economic constraint is that time-and-materials billing can transfer productivity gains to customers, while the large estimated acquisition pool remains uncertain because it is derived rather than directly counted.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate requirements synthesis, architecture options, configuration scripts, connector and test-code generation, data mapping, migration tooling, defect triage, documentation, training materials, ticket resolution, and project reporting. Current coding-agent use demonstrates task delegation, but firm-level Census evidence shows that adoption is often narrow and augmentative rather than labor-reducing. Humans remain central for ambiguous business needs, system tradeoffs, vendor selection, security, stakeholder coordination, access to customer environments, cutover decisions, exception handling, and production sign-off.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed-fee and milestone projects can preserve initial savings through higher throughput or avoided hiring, and scarce expertise may support value pricing. Much professional-services revenue is nevertheless billed by hours, so shorter delivery directly reduces invoices unless capacity is resold, scope expands, or rates rise. Renewals, competitive bids, offshore supply, platform vendors, and customers using the same tools will share the benefit. Durable capture therefore requires packaged delivery methods, measurable quality, faster time to value, and reusable integration assets rather than labor arbitrage alone.
narrative.firm_availability: The industry definition already centers on outsourced customer systems work, so most independent scaled firms should fit the lens, subject to exclusions for resale-led, staffing-led, captive, or nonrepeat businesses. Broad owner aging and active IT-services deal flow indicate succession and buyer interest, but neither establishes a six-digit transfer probability. The frozen firm count suggests a large theoretical pool but is margin-bridged from size classes; customer concentration, founder-owned relationships, thin management, undocumented delivery, and internal succession can materially shrink the actionable set.
narrative.demand_durability: Real output for the broader industry is projected to grow rapidly, supported by cloud adoption, cybersecurity, AI deployment, new software-enabled products, and continuing dependence on customer-specific IT systems. AI creates demand for design and integration even as it automates delivery tasks. The current service basket is durable where legacy systems, regulation, data migration, security, and cross-vendor coordination require accountability, but standardized configurations may migrate to self-service tools, software vendors, or hyperscalers.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The strongest public evidence is four-digit rather than six-digit, and no direct dataset measures wage-weighted AI exposure, realized five-year implementation, LMM billing mix, eligible ownership, or operator displacement. Fast tool change makes current productivity studies unstable, while coding telemetry is selected toward adopters. Client security rules, intellectual-property concerns, hallucinated code, hidden integration dependencies, regression defects, and liability can offset speed gains. Commercially, hourly billing and rapid competitive diffusion can prevent technical productivity from becoming retained profit. The labor input has a vintage mismatch, and the firm-count estimate depends on margin and size-class assumptions.
narrative.principal_driver: Strong growth in customer-specific cloud, cybersecurity, AI, and legacy-integration demand supports repeat outsourced work while AI can compress several expensive digital delivery tasks.
narrative.principal_weakness: Time-and-materials pricing and rapid diffusion of the same tools to competitors, customers, software vendors, and offshore providers can pass through much of the productivity benefit.

sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau NAICS: Computer Systems Design Services
sources.supports: Defines NAICS 541512 as planning and designing integrated computer systems and notes that providers may supply components, install systems, and train or support users.

sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Survey Form for Computer Systems Design and Related Services
sources.supports: Distinguishes systems integration and systems design from adjacent activities and enumerates customer classes and associated products such as packaged-software integration, databases, networks, security, systems design, and integration.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates for Computer Systems Design and Related Services
sources.supports: Provides the broader industry's occupation mix and wages, including computer, software, support, systems-analysis, management, business, and project occupations.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Computer Systems Analysts
sources.supports: Describes customer consultation, technology research, cost-benefit analysis, system configuration, installation, testing, training, domain knowledge, outsourcing, and projected demand.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers
sources.supports: Describes requirements analysis, system design, models, documentation, testing, risk identification, defect reporting, collaboration, security needs, and projected demand.

sources.title: The Microstructure of AI Diffusion: Evidence from Firms, Business Functions, and Worker Tasks
sources.supports: Provides nationally representative evidence on firm AI use, employment-weighted adoption, breadth of functional use, augmentation, and reported employment effects.

sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index: AI's Impact on Software Development
sources.supports: Analyzes coding interactions, automation versus augmentation, common development uses, and differing startup and enterprise representation.

sources.title: METR: We Are Changing Our Developer Productivity Experiment Design
sources.supports: Summarizes controlled developer-productivity results and explains why later estimates are unreliable because of adoption-related selection and measurement problems.

sources.title: BLS Industry and Occupational Employment Projections Overview and Highlights
sources.supports: Projects strong employment growth for computer systems design and related services and attributes demand to infrastructure, custom solutions, cybersecurity, cloud, and AI implementation.

sources.title: BLS Industries with the Fastest Growing Output
sources.supports: Reports projected chained-dollar output and compound annual real growth for four-digit computer systems design and related services.

sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: Reports that more than half of responding owners of U.S. employer businesses were at least age 55, providing a broad succession-pressure proxy.

sources.title: PwC Global M&A Trends in Technology, Media and Telecommunications
sources.supports: Reports increased IT-services share of technology deal activity, notable transactions, and expected continued buyer interest based on LSEG and PwC analysis.

sources.title: Perficient 2023 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Discloses adjacent IT-consulting contract structures and revenue split among time-and-materials, fixed-fee, and fixed-fee-percent-complete professional services.
